about.html: I'm a 21-year-old designer and developer from Porto, Portugal. In 2025, I completed my Bachelor's degree in Multimedia at ESMAD - Escola Superior de Media Artes e Design. I'm currently pursuing a Master's degree in Multimedia at FEUP - Faculdade de Engenharia da Universidade do Porto. With foundations in both art and technology, I am passionate about creating innovative ways to merge the two.

skills.label: Video
skills.items: After Effects, Premiere Pro, DaVinci Resolve, Final Cut Pro

description.left: In collaboration with Ocubo and Alfandega do Porto, this project was developed for the course unit Multimedia. This project's goal was to conceptually explore our visions of a futuristic city.
description.right: Through 2D and 3D animations, mixing the real and the digital, the intention is to take spectators on a journey that explores the limits of human evolution. It is also presented as A Cidade do Futuro / City of the Future.

2.title: Jenkins/5 Shades of Pink
postVideoText: Me and my colleague Alice Logvina once again worked together, and we both worked on all components such as coding in Processing and utilizing the Arduino.

For this project, we used the graphic developments from our midterm work and introduced interactivity through Arduino and a webcam. The webcam, detecting light, generates small triangles in the illuminated area. The cube also rotates depending on the position of the light. In addition, with the Arduino, we had 4 buttons in the center of the room to switch between these components.

left: Jenkins was developed for the course unit Programming III, whose proposal was to create a program in Processing that produces a sequence of generative images to create a real-time video based on the user's sound input.
In other words, the goal was to create a video with a mixed methodology that explores the aesthetic and technical possibilities of Processing.

It was developed entirely in Processing, an open-source programming language and integrated development environment. The code incorporates various inputs: the letters S, D, and H; the arrow keys; the numbers from 1 to 5; the left mouse button; and mouse dragging. By pressing keys 1 to 5, different images from the film Marie Antoinette (2006) appear, overlap, and disappear when the same keys are pressed again.
right: The output includes graphic animations that react to sound, such as a sound wave, triangles generated through mouse movement, circles that appear when the D key is pressed, random geometric shapes generated by clicking the H key, circles that appear with the S key and are removed by the mouse, and circles triggered by the arrow keys.

This project was done in collaboration with my colleague Alice Logvina. We worked together to produce the various components in Processing. She handled the 3D objects like the cube and the terrain, and I handled the programming of the other elements. Later, in collaboration with the Interface Design course unit, this project evolved into an interactive installation using Processing and an Arduino simultaneously, thus creating 5 Shades of Pink.

left: Serpentina was developed for the course unit Digital Games, where we were challenged to add a meaningful twist to a classic arcade title. We chose Snake, a game with a familiar top-down 2D structure and a clear rhythm of movement, growth, and survival, and reimagined that rhythm through a single disruptive mechanic.

Players control the snake with W/A/S/D or arrow keys and collect strawberries that appear randomly inside the arena. Each strawberry increases the snake by one segment, but every three strawberries the controls invert, so up becomes down and left becomes right. That shift forces players to constantly remap their thinking, while still preserving the recognizable Snake loop of avoiding walls and self-collision.

Using the MDA perspective, our goal was to move the aesthetic experience away from steady mastery and toward adaptation, tension, and cognitive disruption. Rather than relying only on increasing snake length for difficulty, we introduced periodic uncertainty and turned movement itself into a strategic decision about timing, positioning, and risk. Even with a relatively small code intervention, the experiential impact was significant, aligning with our intent to make minimal mechanical changes with maximum effect.
right: Technically, we implemented this in Godot's 2D framework with a strawberry counter that triggers inversion at every multiple of three, a dynamic input remapping layer that switches between normal and reversed controls, and state persistence until the next threshold.

Our intended outcomes were to amplify cognitive challenge, introduce productive frustration, and increase replayability by ensuring that inversion moments happen in different spatial contexts each run. We also expected the mechanic to have a cognitive-training quality, since players must interrupt learned motor patterns and continuously rebuild control logic in real time.

Playtesting largely confirmed those goals. Players quickly recognized the mental challenge dimension, and although early runs produced frequent failures during inversion phases, that difficulty increased engagement rather than causing abandonment. Overall, Serpentina achieved the desired transformation: it kept the identity of Snake intact while creating a more adaptive, unpredictable, and cognitively demanding experience.

left: Afterglow was developed in the final semester of the Multimedia degree as an individual project that combines game design, illustration, interface design, animation, and programming. The project explores how a point-and-click structure can support a calm and immersive experience centered on atmosphere rather than urgency.

Set in a visually stylized world inspired by cozy gaming and contemporary visual culture, the game invites players to move through spaces at their own pace, discovering interactions through observation and curiosity. Instead of directing the player through explicit instructions, the experience encourages intuitive exploration.

This approach is reinforced by a mostly diegetic interface, where information emerges from the game world itself. By reducing external UI noise and relying on visual cues, the project prioritizes emotional continuity, detail, and a sense of presence.
right: Mechanically, Afterglow remains intentionally light: players navigate environments, inspect objects, and uncover hidden elements, with progression built around free-form interaction rather than task pressure. The absence of strict goals or tutorials turns replayability into a natural consequence of discovery.

Conceptually, the project also reflects on gender representation, accessibility, and the value of aesthetics in game design. It positions visual sensibility as a legitimate design language and contributes to a broader conversation about more diverse, welcoming forms of play.

As a final outcome, Afterglow became both a personal and academic milestone: a cohesive project that demonstrates technical growth while preserving a clear artistic identity across illustration, design, sound, and interaction.

left: Glitch Box is an interactive installation, inspired by the Nintendo game Super Mario, that allows the spectator to interact with a 3D asset: a surprise box. Every time the spectator touches this box, it plays a sound and disappears, appearing elsewhere, ready to repeat that event. The box can randomly appear in any part of the screen, and the spectator has to move around the entire room to be able to successfully play.

My colleague Alice Logvina and I developed this project together under the guidance of Professor Tiago Dionisio. We chose to use a Kinect in combination with Max and Processing, and created a patch that communicates via OSC. We integrated the 3D patch with the full scene, including elements like the castle, clouds, and the mystery box, which became the centerpiece of the interactive experience.
right: In Max, we were able to associate spheres with the hands, which allowed us to detect movements and generate real-time interactions.

We worked on the collision between objects and the hands, creating distinct sound responses for each one, and adjusted the movement axes for greater realism. To improve the scene lighting, we explored Max documentation and added light parameters that made the environment brighter and more immersive.
The clouds were animated to bring more dynamism to the installation, and to complete the experience, we decided to include the Super Mario music, which plays on a loop throughout the interaction.
